coredata.dc:title: Transforming high-density polyethylene plastic bags into eco-friendly carbon dots for detecting ferric (Fe3+) ions
coredata.dc:creator: Lestari, Ratih
Wahyuningsih, Tutik Dwi
Kamiya, Yuichi
Kartini, Indriana
coredata.dc:description: Eco-friendly transformation of HDPE plastic waste into carbon dots (CDs) has been developed in this work. A green technique involving modified pyrolysis-sonication-hydrothermal processes has successfully synthesized the CDs within a short time frame of 9 h and low concentration oxidant (5 wt% H2O2) with remarkable quantum yield (QY) of up to 16.16 %. The resulting CDs showed good fluorescence stability as exposed to UV light irradiation, various ionic strengths, and extended periods of storage. Furthermore, the CDs exhibit a crystalline graphite-like structure and a consistent range of sizes, from 1.0 to 4.5 nm. Structurally, the produced CDs contain oxygenated groups (carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl) and reveal high selectivity for detecting Fe3+ ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.82 μM. The CDs have also demonstrated good %Recovery of 88.25–95.33 % and %RSD of <5 % as Fe3+ sensors for tap water. The synthesized CDs exhibit tremendous potential as a highly promising Fe3+ ions sensor, particularly capable of detecting low concentrations in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
HDPE
Pyrolysis
Sonication
Hydrothermal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing performance of CDs for Fe3+ ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education Culture Research and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Culture, Research, and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Kementerian Pendidikan, Kebudayaan, Riset, dan Teknologi

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author wishes to thank the Ministry of Education, Culture, Research, and Technology , for the fund through the PMDSU Scholarships (No. 4964/UN1/DITLIT/DIT-LIT/PT/2020 ) and Enhancing International Publication Program (PKPI) 2022 (No. 085.22/E4.4/KU/2022 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectra of synthesized CDs at various (a) hydrothermal and (b) sonication times.
Emission spectra of CDs synthesized at various (a) hydrothermal and (b) sonication times. Inset shows the representative of CD's solution under day- and UV-light.
FTIR spectra of CDs at various synthesis conditions, the HDPE spectrum was included for comparison.
(a-b) TEM images at various scales, and the corresponding (c) size distribution, (d) SAED, and (e) EDX of CDs PS1H4.
The XRD pattern of CDs PS1H4.
(a) FTIR and (b) Raman spectra of CDs PS1H4.
Selectivity of CDs PS1H4 toward Fe3+ in various a) cations, b) anion, and c) NaCl concentration (mM) presence as interference.

(a) Emission spectra for CDs PS1H4 in the Fe3+ presence with various concentrations (μM) and (b) relationship between quenching degree and concentration of Fe3+. The inset shows the calibration curve.
(a) UV–Vis spectra, (b) Zeta potential, (c) FTIR spectra of CDs PS1H4 and CDs PS1H4 interacted with Fe3+.
The proposed structure, synthesis mechanism, and sensing mechanism of CDs.
Parameters for the synthesis of CDs by variation of pyrolysis, sonication, and hydrothermal time.
The quantum yield of CDs synthesized from plastic waste.
The comparison of the sensing performance CDs for Fe3+ to previous research.
The detection of Fe3+ in real tap water samples using the CDs PS1H4 sensor.

head.ce:title: Transforming high-density polyethylene plastic bags into eco-friendly carbon dots for detecting ferric (Fe3+) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, University of Gadjah Mada, Bulak Sumur, 55281 Yogyakarta, Indonesia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, University of Gadjah Mada, Bulak Sumur 55281, Yogyakarta, Indonesia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Environmental Earth Science, Hokkaido University, North 10 West 5, Sapporo 060-0810, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Environmental Earth Science, Hokkaido University, North 10 West 5, Sapporo 060-0810, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Eco-friendly transformation of HDPE plastic waste into carbon dots (CDs) has been developed in this work. A green technique involving modified pyrolysis-sonication-hydrothermal processes has successfully synthesized the CDs within a short time frame of 9 h and low concentration oxidant (5 wt% H2O2) with remarkable quantum yield (QY) of up to 16.16 %. The resulting CDs showed good fluorescence stability as exposed to UV light irradiation, various ionic strengths, and extended periods of storage. Furthermore, the CDs exhibit a crystalline graphite-like structure and a consistent range of sizes, from 1.0 to 4.5 nm. Structurally, the produced CDs contain oxygenated groups (carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl) and reveal high selectivity for detecting Fe3+ ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.82 μM. The CDs have also demonstrated good %Recovery of 88.25–95.33 % and %RSD of <5 % as Fe3+ sensors for tap water. The synthesized CDs exhibit tremendous potential as a highly promising Fe3+ ions sensor, particularly capable of detecting low concentrations in real water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The production of plastics continues to rise globally, reaching 8300 million metric tons (Mt) in 2017. Unfortunately, only 9 % of the plastic produced underwent the recycling process, while 12 % was subjected to incineration, and a staggering 79 % was dumped into the environment in 2015 [1]. If appropriate actions are not implemented, 12,000 Mt. of plastic waste is expected released into the environment by 2050 [2]. Generally, plastic waste will accumulate in water and soil and is antibiotically degraded into nano-, micro-, and macroplastic particles in water and soil [3]. Microplastics can be taken in the body of animals and accumulate especially in higher predators through a food chain. Li et al. [4] reported histopathological damage in fish tissue caused by plastic contamination and that large amounts of microplastic with sizes below 3 mm were found in the intestines in Hemiculter leucisculus. Long-term accumulation of microplastics can cause tissue damage, fibrosis, and cancer in humans [5]. Therefore, the conversion of plastic waste into high-value products, such as carbon dots (CDs) is keenly desired.

CDs are carbon nanomaterials that possess a zero-dimensional structure and have a size smaller than 10 nm [6]. Unlike other carbon materials, CDs exhibit high solubility in water, strong fluorescence, high stability, low toxicity, and are easily modifiable. These exceptional properties make CDs suitable for various applications, including light-emitting diodes [7], solar cells [8], sensors [9], catalysis [10], and bio-imaging [11]. Several methods have been employed to synthesize CDs using different raw materials, such as glucose [12], citric acid [13], biomass [14], and plastic [15]. The use of plastic as a raw material is particularly noteworthy due to its potential to reduce plastic waste. Polyvinyl chloride (PVC), polyethylene terephthalate (PET), low-density polyethylene (LDPE), and high-density polyethylene (HDPE) are common plastics used [16]. Among these, HDPE offers the highest carbon content of 84.74 %, making it a suitable candidate for CDs synthesis [17].

A blue fluorescence CDs with 5.2 % of QY has been synthesized from PET using pyrolysis for 2 h at 300 °C, followed by hydrothermal for 12 h at 180 °C [18] Similarly, Chan and Zichenko et al. [19] produced CDs from PET (QY < 9.1 %) using pyrolysis for 0.5 h at 700 °C and hydrothermal treatment for 12 h at 180 °C. Then, bright-blue color fluorescence CDs with a QY of 31.81 % were obtained by Muro-Hidalgo et al. [20] employing pyrolysis for 2 h at 400 °C and hydrothermal for 8 h at 200 °C. Such high QY is supposed to achieve by the use of L-cysteine and o-phenylenediamine as additional precursors. The pyrolysis-hydrothermal method has gained popularity for synthesizing CDs from plastic, as demonstrated by the aforementioned studies. This method is favored because of the simplicity and ability to produce a high QY of CDs ranging from 5.2 % to 31.81 %. However, it typically requires a relatively long synthesis time of 10–14 h at high temperatures.

A shorter CD synthesis of 4.5 h has been proposed through pyrolysis and sonication with the addition of oxidants [21]. Resulting in blue-fluorescent CDs with a QY of 33 %, the protocol used strong acid, H2SO4 as the oxidant. Since H2SO4 is high toxicity, reactive, and corrosive, the procedure is unfavorable. In this work, we proposed a green synthesis route for CDs from plastic waste, specifically HDPE plastic waste by combining pyrolysis, sonication, and hydrothermal (PSH) with a low concentration of H2O2 as the oxidant. The combination of three techniques was performed to hinder the high-temperature route to achieving high QY CDs (> 5.2 %) and a short synthesis time (〈10h). Then, the resulting CDs were evaluated for cation sensing [18,22–24]. The cation such as Fe3+ plays a significant function in biological processes, including enzymatic reaction and oxygen transportation. Elevated levels of Fe3+ within the human body can cause physiological damage including heart disease, kidney failure, and liver injury [25]. The sensing approach is keenly desired to provide an analysis method that is effortless, uncomplicated, and precise for detecting Fe3+. The use of complex instruments and methods such as colorimetric assay, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) for cation analysis can be avoided.

All experimental procedures used deionized water. The HDPE plastic bags as the carbon source for CDs synthesis were manufactured by Santoso Company, Indonesia. Chemicals such as hydrogen peroxide solution (30 wt% H2O2), sodium chloride (KCl), iron sulfate (FeSO4), calcium nitrate (Ca(NO3)2), mercuric chloride (HgCl2), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), cupric chloride (CuCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), cadmium nitrate (Cd(NO3)2), iron chloride (FeCl3), aluminum nitrate (Al(NO3)3), sodium chloride (NaCl), hydrochloric acid (HCl), quinine sulfate, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium iodide (NaI), sodium bromide (NaBr), and sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) were obtained from Merck (Germany).

CDs synthesis involved several steps, pyrolysis of HDPE plastic bags, followed by sonication and hydrothermal treatment. Initially, small pieces of HDPE plastic bags were cut, and 1 g of these small pieces was heated using a furnace for 4 h at 300 °C to obtain raw carbon. Next, 0.25 g of the raw carbon was combined with 15 mL of a 5 wt% H2O2 solution and subjected to sonication (at 60 W) for different durations, as indicated in Table 1 . The obtained mixture was moved to a Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave and heated at 180 °C for varied durations (as listed in Table 1). After the reaction temperature decreased, the product was centrifugated for 30 min at 4000 rpm to isolate CDs, while removing any remaining residue. The resulting CDs exhibited a light yellow color under ambient conditions and displayed strong greenish-blue fluorescence upon UV irradiation (see Fig. 2).

The stability testing of CDs was conducted using various methods. The pH stability of CDs was assessed using 3 mL of deionized water mixed with 0.1 mL of CDs, then the pH was adjusted to 2–13 by adding either hydrochloric acid or sodium hydroxide solution. The emission spectrum of the solutions was then recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer at 320 nm as the maximum excitation wavelength. For the ion strength stability test, various concentrations of NaCl solutions (0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 M) were prepared. About 3 mL of each NaCl solution was mixed with 0.1 mL of CDs, then characterized using a fluorescence spectrometer. To evaluate the stability of CDs against UV light, 0.1 mL of CDs in 3 mL of deionized water was exposed to UV light for varying durations ranging from 0 to 120 min, with intervals of 20 min. Subsequently, the solution was analyzed using a fluorescence spectrometer at 320 nm as the maximum excitation wavelength. The time stability of CDs was investigated by monitoring the fluorescence intensity changes during a storage period of up to 35 days. For this, the CDs solution (0.1 mL) in 3 mL of distilled water was stored under room conditions, and fluorescence measurements were taken on days 0, 14, 21, and 35.

Using quinine sulfate as a reference with a QY of 54 % [26], the QY of CDs was determined and calculated according to Eq. (1). The symbols “Q” and “I” express the QY and fluorescence intensity, respectively. The parameter “η” denotes the refractive index of solutions, while “A” corresponds to the absorbance. The absorbance of the sample and quinine sulfate was adjusted below 0.1. The subscripts “CDs” and “R” refer to the sample and reference, respectively.
(1)
Q CDs
=
Q R
·
I CDs
/
I R
·
A R
/
A CDs
·
η 2
CDs
/
η 2
R

The selectivity of CDs as a Fe3+ sensor was assessed using 0.2 mL of CDs added with 6 mL of Fe3+, K+, Co2+ , Hg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, and Al3+ solution (400 μM). Analyzing the interference effect to Fe3+ detection was done by mixing Fe3+ solution (400 μM) with various cations (K+, Co2+ , Hg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, and Al3+), anions (Clˉ, Brˉ, Iˉ, SO4 2ˉ, OHˉ), and ionic strength (0–1600 mM of NaCl). The emission spectrum of each solution was then recorded at 320 nm as the maximum excitation wavelength and obtained I/I0, where I is the fluorescence intensity of CDs containing other ions and I0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of CDs without ions addition.

The CDs sensitivity to Fe3+ was also investigated by mixing 0.1 mL of CDs and 3 mL of FeCl3 solutions at various concentrations (0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 200, 300, 400, 600, and 800 μM). The emission spectrum of each solution obtained was then recorded at 320 nm as the maximum excitation wavelength. The ability of CDs as a sensor was applied by detecting Fe3+ ion in tap water. About 0.2 mL of CDs were added with various concentrations of Fe3+ (0, 30, 50, and 80 μM) and 6 mL of tap water, then the fluorescence intensity of each mixture was measured at 320 nm as the maximum excitation wavelength.

Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), and transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were captured by FEI Technai G2 20S-twin TEM operating at 200 kV. Using a LabX XRD-6000 Shimadzu, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired. FTIR spectra were obtained using Shimadzu, IRPrestige-21. A Thermo Scientific Genesys 10S spectrophotometer was employed to measure UV–Vis absorption spectra with the wavelength range from 200 to 800 nm at ambient temperature. For emission spectra, a fluorescence spectrophotometer RF-6000 series (Shimadzu) was used at 270–570 nm and 300 to 600 nm of excitation and emission wavelength, respectively. Malvern Zetasizer Nano Series was used for the zeta potential measurement of CDs. Elemental analysis (CHNS/O) of the CDs was performed using a Flash Smart Elemental Analyzer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). LabRAM HR Evolution-HORIBA was used to obtain Raman spectra of CDs, with 785 nm as the laser wavelength.

The UV–Vis spectra displayed the maximum absorbance peak at approximately 290 nm, irrespective of the synthesis method and conditions employed as presented in Fig. 1 . This absorption was assignable to the π to π* electron transition of the CC group, suggesting that the CDs core sites consisted of these groups. Subsequently, the absorption extended to 400 nm, indicating the presence of carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups on the CDs surface, because such extended absorption appears from n to π* transition of those functional groups [27].

Among the different synthesis methods employed, the pyrolysis-sonication (PS0.5) method resulted in CDs with lower absorbance compared to the pyrolysis-hydrothermal (PH6) as shown in Fig. 1a. This disparity was assigned to the absence of the hydrothermal process, which hinders the optimal formation of CDs cores (C=C) and the conversion of polymer plastic into oxygen-rich functional groups. The validity of this assumption is supported by the FTIR spectra of CDs PS0.5, as depicted in Fig. 3, where no absorption peaks from CC and oxygen functional groups are noticed. However, sonication treatment before the hydrothermal process significantly enhanced the light absorption of CDs (PS0.5H2, PS0.5H4, and PS0.5H6).

Moreover, prolonging the sonication time from 0.5 to 2 h (PS0.5H4, PS1H4, and PS2H4) can increase light absorption by the CDs (Fig. 1b). Notably, CDs synthesized using the PS1H4 and PS2H4 method exhibited significantly higher light absorption than CDs PS0.5H6 and those synthesized using the single hydrothermal method (H4) (Fig. 1b). The enhancement in light absorption suggests an augmented presence of core sites and surface functional groups of CDs. This finding is consistent with FTIR spectra of CDs PS1H4 depicted in Fig. 3, which clearly illustrate the emergence of absorption from OH, CO, CO, O—C—O, and CC. The sonication process induces the generation of both low and high-pressure waves within the solution, causing the formation of small bubbles, intense hydrodynamic shear forces, high-velocity liquid jets, and deagglomeration [28]. Consequently, these effects result in greater disruption of the carbon polymer chain within the plastic, leading to the formation of an intermediate product. Subsequently, this intermediate product undergoes carbonization, ultimately giving rise to the structure of CDs [18]. Additionally, the heat generated by ultrasonic waves can facilitate the oxidation process and increase core sites and surface functional groups containing oxygen, leading to enhanced light absorption [29,30]. In contrast, when CDs were synthesized without the pyrolysis process (S1H4), the resulting CDs displayed significantly lower absorbance compared to those synthesized using the PS1H4 method (Fig. 1b). Pyrolysis may cause the breaking of the plastic polymer chains, leading to the easy formation of the CDs structure.

All of the CDs obtained exhibited the maximum excitation wavelength at 320 nm and emitted light within the visible range (433–445 nm), according to Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2a, the addition of sonication increased the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. Even when the hydrothermal time was reduced to 4 h (PS0.5H4), the fluorescence intensity remained relatively high compared to CDs obtained through 6 h of the hydrothermal process without sonication (PH6). This finding suggests that sonication played a crucial role in promoting both oxidation and carbonization processes, thereby shortening the required hydrothermal time for CDs formation. Furthermore, in Fig. 2a, it is evident that the CDs synthesized using the PS0.5 method exhibited the lowest fluorescence intensity. This is in line with UV–Vis spectra, which is caused by the limited number of surface functional groups, resulting from the omission of the hydrothermal process in this particular approach.

Fig. 2b further presents that the fluorescence intensity increase by extending the sonication time from 0.5 to 2 h (PS0.5H4, PS1H4, and PS2H4), assigned by an augmentation in the functional group's number on the CDs surface, which aligns with the UV–Vis characterization. Sonication facilitates the formation of these functional groups, serving as electron trapping sites [26]. During the process of electron relaxation and recombination, the excess energy is emitted as photons, thereby enhancing the fluorescence intensity. However, the fluorescence intensity was not significant difference with conducting sonication for 1 h (PS1H4) or 2 h (PS2H4). Consequently, PS1H4 was selected as a representative of high-quality CDs and was further subjected to characterization. In contrast, the single hydrothermal method (H4) resulted in low fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2b), while CDs synthesized using the S1H4 method exhibited higher fluorescence intensity compared to H4 (Fig. 2b). Although the use of sonication and hydrothermal without pyrolysis (S1H4) produced fairly good fluorescence intensity, it did not surpass the CDs synthesized using the PS1H4 method (Fig. 2b). The UV–Vis spectra supported the observation that pyrolysis plays a crucial role in promoting the CDs structure creation. Consequently, the combination of three methods—pyrolysis, sonication, and hydrothermal (PSH) proves to be highly advantageous for synthesizing CDs with enhanced fluorescence intensity.

Altering the excitation wavelength (280–420 nm) resulted in the emission wavelength shift from CDs obtained using various methods (PH, PSH, PS, and single hydrothermal) as displayed in Fig. S1, indicating the distinctive characteristic of excitation-dependent emission from CDs. This characteristic is commonly found in CDs. Previous research suggests the excitation-dependent emission behavior was connected to the variations in particle sizes or functional groups on CDs surface [31]. Furthermore, the comparison of plastic-based CDs QY in this study with previous research was presented in Table 2 . The QY of the CDs obtained using the PSH method is generally higher than those obtained through pyrolysis-hydrothermal, hydrothermal, sonication-hydrothermal, and pyrolysis methods using plastic waste as the raw material, except for the CDs produced by Muro-Hidalgo et al. [20]. It is important to mention that they employed additional chemicals (L-cysteine and o-phenylenediamine), which could potentially contribute to the variation in surface functional groups and improve CDs QY. The high QY observed in this study suggests that the CDs exhibit strong fluorescence signals, enhancing their potential as highly sensitive and accurate sensors.

CDs PS1H4 demonstrate exceptional stability against UV light, storage conditions, and varying ionic strengths. The fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength remained unchanged even after being exposed to UV irradiation for up to 120 min (Fig. S2), stored for 35 days (Fig. S3), and subjected to different concentrations of NaCl (Fig. S4). This remarkable stability is particularly advantageous for CDs applications, requiring direct interaction with UV light (such as sensors) and utilization in severe conditions (such as seawater) [26]. Additionally, the variation in pH caused the emission wavelengths of CDs PS1H4 to remain constant, displayed in Fig. S5. However, the fluorescence intensity was notably affected. CDs contain the carboxyl group that is highly sensitive to pH fluctuations. A noteworthy rise in the intensity of fluorescence was observed at pH 9 or higher (pH 9–12). This enhancement was caused by the negative charge from carboxylate groups on the CDs surface. This behavior is similar to that reported for CDs on TiO2 [33]. At alkaline pH levels (pH 9–12), the hydrogen in the carboxylic acid group dissociates to form H+, resulting in a negatively charged in the CDs surface. Consequently, electrostatic repulsion occurs between the CDs, causing an increase in fluorescence intensity [34]. The favorable interaction of CDs PS1H4 in both acidic and alkaline environments indicates their potential application as pH sensors.

The mechanism underlying the conversion of HDPE plastic polymers into CDs using the PSH method was investigated through UV–Vis and FTIR spectroscopy. The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. S6) revealed that extending the sonication time up to 1 h and employing the hydrothermal method for 4 h enhanced light absorption by the CDs. This enhancement was due to an increase in core sites containing sp2 CC bonds and surface sites containing oxygen groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl). The FTIR spectra (Fig. 3 ) provided further insights into the mechanism. After pyrolysis treatment, the absorption bands corresponding to sp3 CH stretching at 2915 and 2850 cm−1 weakened, along with the CH2 bending at 1470 cm−1. These changes indicated the breaking of polymeric chains through pyrolysis [35]. Sonication for 0.5 h (PS0.5) further decreased the absorption of CH and -CH2 bonds, suggesting a significant breakage of polymeric bonds from the plastic. After 1 h of sonication (PS1), new absorption bands corresponding to sp3 CH stretching, CC stretching vibrations, and CH bending appeared at 2848, 1640, and 1367 cm−1, respectively, indicating the formation of CC units and carbonization. Subsequent hydrothermal treatment for 4 h (PS1H4) resulted in the appearance of absorption bands corresponding to OH stretching, CO stretching, CO stretching, and O—CO bending vibrations at 3350, 1765, 1210, and 670 cm−1, respectively. These bands indicated the abundant oxygen functional groups present on the CDs surface [17]. According to these results, a proposed mechanism of CDs synthesis is presented in Fig. 10, which will be explained in detail later.

The structure of CDs PS1H4 was characterized using TEM, XRD, FTIR, and Raman spectroscopy. TEM imaging (Fig. 4a) revealed that the CDs exhibited spherical shapes and were well dispersed. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) analysis (Fig. 4b) showed lattice fringes with distances of 0.21 and 0.35 nm attributable to (110) and 0.35 nm (002) planes of the crystalline graphite-like structure, respectively. The slightly larger (002) lattice for the CDs PS1H4 compared to normal graphite (0.34 nm) suggested the abundant functional groups at the CDs edges, causing lattice expansion [18]. An additional lattice fringe at 0.28 nm was observed, corresponding to (003) of graphite, indicating the presence of a crystalline graphite-like structure within the CDs core. The distribution of CDs size was determined (Fig. 4c), CDs exhibit a consistent range of sizes, from 1.0 to 4.5 nm, with 2.3 nm as the average size. The SAED pattern (Fig. 4d) confirmed the lattice spacing of 0.35 nm (002) and 0.21 nm (110) of graphite, in line with the TEM observations. EDS analysis (Fig. 4e) indicated that the synthesized CDs contained carbon and oxygen. This finding is strengthened using elemental analysis, the CDs PS1H4 contain 65.98 % oxygen, 27.08 % carbon, and 5.06 % hydrogen. CDs PS1H4 contain higher oxygen contents than pyrolisis products, which contain 3.74 % oxygen, 83.38 % carbon, and 12.88 % hydrogen. It is indicated that the sonication and hydrothermal processes increased the oxygen group number on the CDs surface compared to the pyrolysis products, as reported in previous studies [36].

The XRD pattern as displayed in Fig. 5 exhibited an amorphous nature, which was attributed to the CDs surface containing the abundance of functional groups. The observed peak at 25.62° corresponded to (002) reflection of graphite, with 0.35 nm as interlayer spacing (JCPDS 01–075-1621). The slightly larger interlayer spacing compared to normal graphite (0.34 nm) is likely caused by oxygen groups present on the carbon layer structure. Additionally, a broad peak at 2θ = 11.1° indicated an interlayer spacing of 0.80 nm, characteristic of graphite oxide (GO) [37]. Another signal at 19.4° suggested that GO was not completely interconnectivity with oxygen atoms [38]. Furthermore, a peak at 30° could be indexed to the (003) plane of graphite [18,39]. These XRD results were consistent with the TEM analysis, confirming that the CDs structure consisted of the (002) and (003) planes of graphite. This structure is commonly found in CDs [18,26].

In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 6a), characteristic absorption bands corresponding to different vibrations were observed, indicating the various functional groups in the CDs. Stretching vibration of OH and sp3 CH appeared at 3345 and 2891–2776 cm−1, respectively. The bending vibration of CH at 1397 cm−1, the CO, CC, and CO stretching vibrations were evident at 1260, 1527, and 1703 cm−1, respectively [36]. It confirmed the existence of oxygen groups, sp3 and sp2 carbons in the CDs. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 6b exhibited two prominent bands, namely the G and D bands, located at 1619 and 1380 cm−1, respectively. Moreover, the CO peak was observed at 1851 cm−1 [40]. The G band corresponds to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a crystalline lattice and is primarily observed in graphitic materials. These observations further confirm the presence of a graphite structure in the CDs PS1H4. On the other hand, the D band arises due to the presence of sp3-hybridized carbon atoms or structural defects in the sp2 graphite-like microdomains. Overall, the FTIR, Raman, TEM, and XRD analyses provide consistent evidence that the CDs PS1H4 possess a graphite structure at the core site, while the surface is enriched with abundant oxygen groups including carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl. These findings are depicted in Fig. 10, illustrating the structural composition of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of CDs for Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 7a, only Fe3+ ions significantly quench the fluorescence of CDs, while the fluorescence intensity remained constant when exposed to other cations, demonstrating the exceptional selectivity of the CDs PS1H4 for Fe3+ ions. Further evidence, the addition of different cations (K+, Co2+ , Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Al3+), anions (Clˉ, Brˉ, Iˉ, SO4 2ˉ, OHˉ), and variations ionic strength (0–1600 mM of NaCl) to the CDs-Fe3+ mixture did not cause a significant change in fluorescence intensity compared to CDs-Fe3+ as seen in Fig. 7a-c. This indicates that CDs exhibit good selectivity toward Fe3+. This finding aligns with Hu et al. [18] study, which also stated the remarkable selectivity of CDs toward Fe3+. The selectivity arises from the complexation ability between Fe3+ ions and surface functional groups on the CDs, including CO and CO. The strong interaction between these leads to a robust chelation effect or coordination reaction, ultimately resulting in the quenching of fluorescence [41]. The high selectivity of the CDs to Fe3+ ions can be caused by the relative positioning of the n–π∗ states from CO in the CDs above the metal-centered d-d states from Fe3+ [42]. This specific arrangement allows for effective energy transfer and interaction between the functional groups and Fe3+ ions, leading to the observed fluorescence quenching behavior.

Fig. 8a presents the gradual decrease of fluorescence intensity as the Fe3+ concentration increased from 0 to 800 μM. Notably, the quenching degree exhibited a linear increase with Fe3+ concentrations below 60 μM, displaying a high correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9991 (inset in Fig. 8b). Based on the 3σ/S (where σ is the standard deviation of the response curve and S is the slope of the calibration curve), the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.82 μM, which is below the maximum allowable Fe3+ concentration (5.4 μM or 0.3 mg L−1) in drinking water as set by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). Furthermore, Table 3 showed that this work is comparable with previous research to detect Fe3+. Enhancing the QY of CDs can further increase the sensitivity, which corresponds to a stronger fluorescence signal generated by the CDs. Some current studies have established the range of standard curves of Fe3+ solutions in the nanomolar scale, resulting in LOD achieved at the nanomolar unit (70 nM) [44,45]. In contrast, our study employed a micromolar scale (0, 10, 20, 40, and 60 μM), causing a LOD that may not be in the nanometer range but is still below the maximum EPA-permitted concentration of Fe3+. Overall, CDs PS1H4 demonstrates reliable Fe3+ detection capabilities, offering advantages in terms of simplicity and cost-effectiveness compared to existing methods such as AAS, HPLC, and colorimetric assay.

Moreover, the detection of Fe3+ ions using CDs PS1H4 was successfully carried out on real water samples, specifically tap water. To prepare the tap water samples, they were spiked with varying concentrations of Fe3+ metal ions. The measurements were repeated twice and the analysis error was indicated by the relative standard deviations (RSD). Table 4 showed the good %Recovery of Fe3+ ions in tap water samples ranging from 88.25 % to 95.33 % [46]. Additionally, %RSD was found to be <5 %. These results demonstrate the accuracy and reliability of the Fe3+ analysis using the CDs PS1H4 sensor. The successful application of the CDs PS1H4 sensor on real water samples (tap water) provides highlights of its potential as an effective tool for Fe3+ ion detection.

The possible mechanism of CDs PS1H4 to detect Fe3+ has been studied using UV–Vis, zeta potential, and FTIR characterization. The UV–Vis spectra in Fig. 9a reveal a noticeable change in light absorption with Fe3+ addition to the CDs PS1H4, indicating an interaction between the two. This interaction was caused by the negatively charged groups on the CDs surface, which can bind with Fe3+ to form CDs-Fe3+ complexes. To further investigate this interaction, zeta potential characterization (Fig. 9b) was performed. It is observed that the CDs PS1H4 exhibit a negative zeta potential owing to the abundance of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the CDs surface, resulting in a negatively charged particle [47]. However, upon Fe3+ addition, the zeta potential of the CDs PS1H4 shifts toward a positive value. This shift indicates the presence of an interaction between the positively charged Fe3+ ions and the negatively charged on the CDs surface. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 9c) provide additional insights into the interaction between the CDs PS1H4 and Fe3+. The spectra display a shift in the absorption bands related to CO, suggesting their involvement in the binding process with Fe3+. Furthermore, a reduction intensity of CO, CH, CC, and CO absorption bands are observed, indicating their participation in the interaction with Fe3+. The Fe3+ can bind with CDs surface groups containing CO and CO. These findings are in line with previous studies [22], indicating that the CDs PS1H4 form complexes with Fe3+ through the CO and CO groups. In addition, the CC group in the CDs core also can interact with Fe3+ causing the reduction absorption of CC and CH in FTIR spectra, consistent with Hu et al. studies [18].

According to the characterization above, we propose a plausible mechanism to explain the fluorescence quenching between Fe3+ and CDs PS1H4, as illustrated in Fig. 10 . When Fe3+ is present, it leads to the complexes formed between Fe3+ with the CO, CO, and CC. This complexation process causes the d orbitals splitting of Fe3+ ions. Consequently, the electrons from the excited state of the CDs undergo partial transfer to the d orbitals of Fe3+, causing the recombination of electrons and hindering the emission of energy, ultimately resulting in fluorescence quenching [48].

A modified pyrolysis-sonication-hydrothermal (PSH) method was successfully applied to synthesize CDs from HDPE with a shorter synthesis time. The addition of sonication during the synthesis process increased the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield up to 16.16 %. The CDs obtained with optimal conditions, namely CDs PS1H4, had a size of 2.26 nm and a core site with graphite structure, with abundant oxygen functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl) on the surface. The mechanism of HDPE conversion into CDs involved several stages, including the breaking of the carbon‑carbon bond in the plastic polymer at the pyrolysis stage, carbonization at the sonication stage, and surface passivation to prevent aggregation at the hydrothermal stage. The resulting CDs PS1H4 were highly stable against long-term storage, UV light, and ionic strength. Those advantageous properties made the CDs applicable for Fe3+ ions sensor with a LOD of 3.82 μM via a quenching mechanism. Fe3+ can interact with CO and CO on the CDs surface to form a complex, and quenching the fluorescence. CDs PS1H4 have shown good %Recovery ranging from 88.25 % to 95.33 % and %RSD <5 % for detecting ferric ions in tap water.

Ratih Lestari: Formal analysis, investigation, methodology, data curation, writing-original draft, and editing. Tutik Dwi Wahyuningsih: Formal analysis, writing-review & editing. Yuichi Kamiya: Formal analysis, writing-review & editing. Indriana Kartini: Conceptualization, supervision, project administration, final writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author wishes to thank the Indonesia Ministry of Education, Culture, Research, and Technology, for the fund through the PMDSU Scholarships (No. 4964/UN1/DITLIT/DIT-LIT/PT/2020) and Enhancing International Publication Program (PKPI) 2022 (No. 085.22/E4.4/KU/2022).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110271.
